target.name: Pentacyclic Framework of Kopsia Alkaloids (Intermediate 22)

key_motifs: carbon bridge between C2 and C20

target.motivation: Pyrroloazocine alkaloids from the Kopsia genus exhibit structural complexity and pharmacological potential. The synthesis of these alkaloids remains challenging due to the complexity of their core pentacyclic structures and the need for precise stereochemical control. This study aims to provide a versatile platform for the divergent synthesis of diverse Kopsia alkaloids.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Unified total synthesis of pyrroloazocine indole alkaloids
Concise and Versatile Synthesis of Alkaloids from Kopsia tenuis: Total Synthesis of (±)-Lundurine A and B

Synthetic Studies toward Lapidilectine-Type Kopsia Alkaloids
Total Synthesis of (−)-Lundurine A and Determination of its Absolute Configuration

success_criteria.free_text: Asymmetric synthesis of the pentacyclic core of pyrroloazocine alkaloids, providing a versatile platform for divergent synthesis of several members.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C16 carboxyl group from exomethylene group in 10
key_disconnections.reasoning: To introduce the proper stereochemistry required for the synthesis of the targeted natural products.
To establish the 2,2-disubstitution pattern and construct the carbon bridge.
To construct the eight-membered C ring of the tetracyclic core.
To achieve asymmetric access to intermediate 12 from bicyclic L-proline derivative 14, involving diastereoselective N-quaternization and stereospecific [2,3]-Stevens rearrangement.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthetic route originates from L-proline and aims to access all three structural variants of pyrroloazocine alkaloids. The strategy involves a C-N-C chirality transfer process for enantioselective α-tertiary amine formation, an eight-membered C ring-forming Friedel-Crafts reaction at the indole C3 position, and a dearomative indole-Claisen rearrangement to form a bridged pentacyclic scaffold. This Claisen-based transformation is novel for pyrroloazocine alkaloid synthesis.

thought.background: Pyrroloazocine alkaloids are a distinct subfamily of Kopsia alkaloids with complex structures and pharmacological potential. Previous synthetic strategies often involve spiro[cyclohexane-2-indoline] or indole-fused azocine intermediates.
thought.gap: Few reports describe divergent strategies to furnish multiple subtype alkaloids from a common intermediate. A novel approach to construct the eight-membered C ring and the C2-C20 carbon bridge is needed.
thought.decision: To develop a concise and asymmetric synthesis of the pyrroloazocine indole core using L-proline as a chiral pool starting material, focusing on an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction for the C ring and a dearomative indole-Claisen rearrangement for the carbon bridge.

evidence.text_span:
P yrroloazocine alkaloids constitute a distinct subfamily of Kopsia alkaloids, predominantly isolated from species within the Kopsia genus, such as Kopsia grandifolia and Kopsia tenuis.1 The defining structural motif of these alkaloids is an indole-fused pyrroloazocine core (Figure 1A). This core adopts a rigid tetracyclic framework that incorporates a carbon bridge between C2 and C20. Individual members further display structural variations such as lactone, diester, or cyclopropane motifs, as exemplified by lapidilectines,2,3 grandilodines,4 lundurines, and tenuisines (1−8).5 The Echavarren group proposed that these alkaloids are biosynthetically interconnected via the carbocation intermediate A, formed through oxidative decarboxylation of the C21 ester group of the diester subtype.6 However, the biosynthetic origin of this pathway has not been fully established experimentally. Preliminary biological studies have shown that pyrroloazocine family alkaloids, such as grandilodine A (1) and lapidilectine B (5), can reverse multidrug resistance in vincristine-resistant cancer cells, underscoring their considerable potential for pharmaceutical development.4 Beyond these promising biological activities, the highly intricate and architecturally unique structures of these alkaloids have drawn sustained attention from the synthetic community.
The synthesis of these alkaloids remains challenging due to the complexity of their core pentacyclic structures and the need for precise stereochemical control. These challenges have spurred the development of many synthetic tactics over the past decades.6−22 As a result, several elegant total syntheses and innovative synthetic strategies have been reported, many of which have been comprehensively reviewed in the literature.23 Most synthetic strategies differ in their approaches to constructing the indole-fused azabicyclo[4.2.2] skeleton B.
These strategies can be broadly categorized into two types: those proceeding through a spiro[cyclohexane-2-indoline] intermediate C and those involving an indole-fused azocine intermediate D (Figure 1B). Even though many novel total syntheses of pyrroloazocine alkaloids have been developed, only a few reports describe divergent strategies that furnish multiple subtype alkaloids from a common intermediate.6,14 As part of our ongoing program on the asymmetric synthesis of $\alpha$ - tertiary amine-containing natural products from chiral amino acids,24 we developed a concise synthesis of the pyrroloazocine indole core with a carbon bridge between C2 and C20, which may provide access to several members of this alkaloid family. Our synthetic strategy differs from previous approaches in that the eight-membered C ring of the tetracyclic core was constructed through an intramolecular Friedel−Crafts reaction of the indole moiety and the C2−C20 carbon bridge was installed through a dearomative indole−Claisen rearrangement (Figure 1C). Notably, this Claisen-based transformation has not, to our knowledge, been previously applied to the synthesis of pyrroloazocine alkaloids. In this report, we describe our efforts toward the asymmetric synthesis of pentacyclic core of pyrroloazocine alkaloids using L-proline as a chiral pool starting material.

thought.background: The goal is to synthesize the pentacyclic core of Kopsia alkaloids. A common intermediate (9) is targeted, which can be transformed into various functional groups for divergent synthesis.
thought.gap: Need to define the key disconnections and transformations to reach the target from readily available starting materials like L-proline and indole.
thought.decision: To disconnect the C16 carboxyl group from an exomethylene group in 10, access 10 from 11 via a dearomative indolic-sigmatropic rearrangement (Claisen), synthesize 11 from 12 via an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction, and obtain 12 from 14 and 15 via a C-N-C chirality transfer strategy involving N-alkylation and Stevens rearrangement.

evidence.text_span:
We devised a synthetic route originating from L-proline, which enables access to all three structural variants of pyrroloazocine alkaloids. Retrosynthetic analysis of the target alkaloids, based on simplification of their functional groups, identified compound 9 as a common intermediate (Figure 2) corresponding to the cationic intermediate A in Figure 1A. We envisioned that the $\Delta ^ { 6 , 7 }$ double bond in 9 can be readily transformed into various functional groups leading to a divergent synthesis of several members. The carboxyl group at C16 of 9 was planned to be introduced from the exomethylene group in 10 with the proper stereochemistry required for the synthesis of the targeted natural products. We envisioned that the pentacyclic intermediate 10 could be accessed from 11 through a dearomative indolic-sigmatropic rearrangement,25,26 which establishes the 2,2-disubstitution
pattern and thereby constructs the carbon bridge between C2 and C20. The planned sequence involved a Tebbe olefination of the ester functionality, followed by a C−C bond-forming [3,3]-Claisen rearrangement.
Advanced intermediate 11 was planned to be synthesized by adopting our previously developed protocol, which involves sequential steps such as diastereoselective N-alkylation, stereospecific Stevens rearrangement, and an intramolecular Friedel−Crafts reaction.27 More specifically, the construction of 11 was designed to proceed through an intramolecular Friedel−Crafts reaction of cyclic O-acyl acetal 12. The asymmetric access to 12 from the bicyclic L-proline derivative 14 was based on the concept of our C−N−C chirality transfer,28 encompassing a diastereoselective N-quaternization
of 14 with indole allylic halide $\mathbf { 14 } / 1 5  1 3 $ ) followed by a stereospecific [2,3]-Stevens rearrangement $1 3  1 2$ ).

thought.background: The synthesis requires indole-derived allyl bromide 15. A known indole boronic ester 16 can be used as a starting material.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize 18 from 16 via Suzuki cross-coupling.
thought.decision: To perform a Suzuki cross-coupling of indole boronic ester 16 with vinyl iodide 17 to afford C2-substituted indole 18.

outcome.observation: C2-substituted indole 18 was obtained in good yield.

evidence.text_span: Our synthetic efforts commenced with the preparation of indole-derived allyl bromide 15 and bicyclic proline derivative 14 (Scheme 1). Azabicycle 14 was prepared from L-proline in two-steps according to our established protocol.27 For the synthesis of allyl bromide 15, the known indole boronic ester 16 was first prepared from indole by following the protocol reported by Hartwig and co-worker.29 The boronic ester 16 was subjected to Suzuki cross-coupling conditions with the known vinyl iodide $\mathbf { 17 } ^ { 3 0 }$ to afford C2-substituted indole 18.

thought.background: Intermediate 18 has been synthesized. The natural products contain a methyl carbamate (Moc) group.
thought.gap: Need to introduce the Moc group onto 18.
thought.decision: To introduce the methyl carbamate group onto 18 using NaH and ClCO2Me.

outcome.observation: The methyl carbamate group was introduced in high yield.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, the methyl carbamate (Moc) group, which is found in the natural products, was introduced in high yield.

thought.background: Intermediate 19 has the Moc group. The next step is to prepare allyl bromide 15.
thought.gap: Need to remove the THP protecting group and then perform an Appel reaction to convert the alcohol to a bromide.
thought.decision: To remove the tetrahydropyranyl (THP) protecting group using HCl, followed by an Appel reaction using PPh3 and CBr4 to afford allyl bromide 15.

outcome.observation: Allyl bromide 15 was obtained in good overall yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the tetrahydropyranyl (THP) protecting group and the subsequent Appel reaction afforded allyl bromide 15 in good overall yield.

thought.background: Allyl bromide 15 and bicyclic proline derivative 14 are prepared. The next step is the stereoselective Cα-alkylation of 14 with 15 to form ammonium salt 13, followed by a [2,3]-Stevens rearrangement to form 12.
thought.gap: Need to optimize the conditions for N-allylation and subsequent Stevens rearrangement.
thought.decision: To perform N-allylation of 14 with 15 in MeCN, followed by treatment of the crude ammonium salt 13 with KOtBu in CH2Cl2 at -78 °C for the [2,3]-Stevens rearrangement.

outcome.observation: Ammonium salt 13 was afforded as the sole detectable diastereomer, and the subsequent Stevens rearrangement furnished α-tertiary amine 12 in good overall yield with high diastereomeric ratio and enantiomeric excess.

evidence.text_span: Next, we explored a stereoselective $_ { \mathbb { C } \alpha }$ -alkylation of 14 with 15 to access the $\alpha$ -tertiary amine-containing product 12 via our C−N−C chirality transfer strategy. $N$ -Allylation of 14 with allyl bromide 15 proceeded sluggishly at room temperature; however, after 5 days, ammonium salt 13 was afforded as the sole detectable diastereomer. Evaporation of the MeCN solvent followed by treatment of the crude 13 with KOtBu in $\mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ at $- 7 8 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ effected a [2,3]-Stevens rearrangement, furnishing $\alpha$ -tertiary amine 12 in $8 0\%$ overall yield with 39:1 diastereomeric ratio and ${ > } 9 8\%$ ee. This two-step sequence could also be executed in a one-pot fashion by adding ${ \mathrm { K } } _ { 2 } { \mathrm { C O } } _ { 3 }$ during the N-allylation, providing 12 in $6 3\%$ yield $3 7\%$ RSM) with ${ > } 9 8\%$ ee. The observed stereochemical outcome is consistent with our earlier studies, which suggest that the reaction proceeds via a Curtin−Hammett-controlled diastereoselective $N .$ -alkylation, followed by a stereospecific [2,3]- Stevens rearrangement.27 The high enantiopurity of the product indicates that the $_ { \mathbb { C } \alpha }$ -chirality of L-proline was efficiently conserved through the $\mathrm { C-N-C }$ chirality transfer process, in accordance with our previous findings.24,27

thought.background: Intermediate 12 is available. The next step is to construct the eight-membered C ring via an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction.
thought.gap: Need to find an effective acid catalyst for the Friedel-Crafts reaction.
thought.decision: To treat acetal 12 with MsOH as an acid catalyst in CH2Cl2.

outcome.observation: Intermediate 11 was obtained in good yield. This is the first example of an eight-membered ring-forming Friedel-Crafts reaction at the indole C3. A trace amount of the seven-membered isomer 20 was also detected.

evidence.text_span: With intermediate 12 in hand, we turned to an intramolecular Friedel−Crafts reaction to construct the eightmembered C ring. Treatment of acetal 12 with MsOH as an acid catalyst in $\mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { C l } _ { 2 }$ afforded 11 in $7 9\%$ yield, whose structure was confirmed by X-ray crystallography. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of an eightmembered ring-forming Friedel−Crafts reaction at the indole C3. A trace amount of the seven-membered isomer 20 was also detected, the formation of which might proceed via an intramolecular Prins-type reaction of the methylene group with an oxonium intermediate,31 followed by migration of the ester group (see Supporting Information) 32

thought.background: An intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction of 12 with MsOH yielded 11 as the major product and a trace of 20. The goal is to selectively form the eight-membered ring product 11.
thought.gap: Using p-TsOH as the acid catalyst might lead to different regioselectivity.
thought.decision: To use p-toluenesulfonic acid monohydrate as the acid catalyst for the intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction of 12.

outcome.observation: The seven-membered isomer 20 became the predominant product, with >20:1 dr. This was not the desired eight-membered ring product 11.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, this isomer became the predominant product when $p$ -toluenesulfonic acid monohydrate was employed as the acid catalyst. The structure of 20 was unambiguously established by single-crystal X-ray crystallographic analysis of its nitrobenzoyl derivative.33

thought.background: Intermediate 11, the pyrroloazocine core, has been established. The next goal is to construct the carbon bridge with C20 ester and indole functionalities using a dearomative indole-Claisen rearrangement.
thought.gap: The Claisen rearrangement requires an enol ether functionality, which can be introduced via Tebbe olefination of the ester in 11.
thought.decision: To perform Tebbe olefination on 11 to introduce the enol ether functionality, yielding 21.

outcome.observation: The desired enol ether 21 was obtained in high yield, but with limited stability under ambient conditions.

evidence.text_span: After establishing the pyrroloazocine core, we directed our efforts toward constructing the carbon bridge with C20 ester and indole functionalities. We envisioned that a C−C bondforming dearomative indole−Claisen rearrangement would provide a direct means to achieve this connectivity.26,34 The Tebbe olefination of 11 introduced the requisite enol ether functionality for the rearrangement,35 affording the desired enol ether 21 in high yield, albeit with limited stability under ambient conditions (Scheme 2).

thought.background: Enol ether 21 has been synthesized but is unstable. It needs to undergo a [3,3]-sigmatropic Claisen rearrangement to form 10, followed by reduction to 22.
thought.gap: Need to perform the rearrangement and subsequent reduction in situ due to the instability of intermediates.
thought.decision: To immediately heat 21 in a sealed tube to enable the [3,3]-sigmatropic Claisen rearrangement to furnish 10, and then directly reduce the crude mixture in situ with NaBH4 in MeOH to deliver the pentacyclic product 22.

outcome.observation: The pentacyclic product 22 was delivered in good overall yield. The structure of 22 was unambiguously confirmed by single-crystal X-ray analysis.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, 21 was immediately heated in a sealed tube, enabling the [3,3]- sigmatropic Claisen rearrangement to furnish rearranged product 10. The formation of 10 can be rationalized by the concerted suprafacial [3,3]-sigmatropic shift, which installs the carbon bridge while simultaneously effecting dearomatization of the indole nucleus. The rearranged product 10 was also somewhat unstable under ambient conditions. Thus, the crude mixture was directly reduced in situ with ${ \mathrm { N a B H } } _ { 4 }$ in MeOH, thereby delivering the pentacyclic product 22 in $8 4\%$ overall yield. The structure of 22 was unambiguously confirmed by single-crystal X-ray analysis of its ether derivative.36 Con-

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield from L-proline to 22 is 6 steps, or 9 steps from indole.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: One failed attempt with p-TsOH for Friedel-Crafts reaction.

validation.significance: This study developed a stereoselective asymmetric synthesis of the pyrroloazocine core of Kopsia alkaloids, leveraging a C-N-C chirality transfer strategy, a novel intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction, and a Claisen rearrangement. This concise route provides a foundation for the synthesis of several pyrroloazocine natural products and expands the synthetic toolkit for complex alkaloid scaffolds with precise control.

source.title: Asymmetric Synthesis of the Pentacyclic Framework of <i>Kopsia</i> Alkaloids.
source.authors: Song Y, Park J, Kim S.
